steps.title: Mission Week Begins: Project Management
steps.body:
Welcome to Week 4 — the Mission Phase.

You've built your AI toolkit. Now it's time to deploy. Over the next 7 days, you'll learn how AI transforms entire disciplines — not just individual tasks.

Today: Project Management. AI isn't just for writing. It's your new project coordinator, sprint planner, risk analyst, and stakeholder communicator.

Let's make AI your PM partner.

The best project managers don't just track tasks — they predict problems before they happen.

AI turns project management from reactive firefighting into proactive orchestration. With the right prompts, your AI can surface schedule risks, flag resource conflicts, and suggest mitigations before a single deadline slips.

steps.title: Scenario: The Project Manager's Reality
steps.body:
You're leading a 12-week product launch. Three teams across two time zones. Stakeholder reports due every Friday.

One team is behind on their milestone. The budget is 15% over. You have 30 minutes to prepare a status update for the CEO.

Without AI, you'd dig through spreadsheets, Slack messages, and Jira tickets. With AI, you can synthesize everything in minutes.

How do you start?

steps.question: What's the most effective first step for your AI-powered PM routine?
options: Ask AI to build a project timeline from scratch without any data
Feed AI your existing project data (tasks, deadlines, dependencies) and ask it to identify risks
Ignore your existing data and start fresh with AI-generated plans
Ask AI to email your team for status updates
feedback: AI needs context. Without your real data, it can't spot actual risks or dependencies.
Exactly. AI works best when grounded in your real project data. Feed it the facts, then let it analyze.
Your existing data is valuable context. AI builds on it — it doesn't replace it.
Let's not give AI your Slack credentials just yet. Start with data, not integrations.

steps.question: AI can replace the need for regular human status check-ins with team members.
feedback: Correct. AI augments project management — it doesn't replace human conversations. The best PMs use AI to prepare for check-ins, not avoid them.
Think again. AI can synthesize status data and surface issues, but team morale, blockers, and interpersonal dynamics require human conversation.

steps.title: AI Sprint Planning Prompt
steps.body:
Here's a prompt template that turns AI into your sprint planning partner:

"I'm planning our next sprint. Here's what we completed last sprint: [list]. Here are our priorities: [list]. Team capacity is [X] hours across [N] people. Available skill sets: [skills].

1. Recommend the optimal sprint backlog
2. Flag any dependencies between items
3. Estimate effort using past velocity data
4. Suggest how to allocate work based on team skills
5. Identify which items have the highest risk of slipping"

steps.question: The AI PM approach can be summarized as: 'Don't just track. _____ .'
feedback: Perfect. The power of AI in project management isn't tracking what's done — it's predicting what's coming.
Close — think about the proactive advantage AI gives you. What does it do that a spreadsheet can't?

steps.question: When using AI for risk assessment, which approach yields the best results?
options: Ask AI to list generic project risks from its training data
Give AI your project plan and ask it to identify specific risks based on scope, timeline, and dependencies
Ignore risks until they become issues
Only ask AI about technical risks, ignore team and process risks
feedback: Generic risks apply to every project and tell you nothing new. Context matters.
Right. The best risk analysis comes from feeding AI your actual project parameters and asking it to think like an experienced PM.
That's how fires start. AI can help you spot risks early — use it.
Risk is multidimensional. Technical, team, timeline, budget — AI can assess all of them when you give it full context.

steps.title: Your Turn: Plan a Sprint with AI
steps.body:
You have a 2-week sprint coming up. Your team has 40 hours of capacity. Priorities: feature X (16h estimate), bug fixes (8h), documentation (4h), code review (6h). Slack time = 6h.

Ask your AI to build you a sprint plan that accounts for dependencies, capacity, and risk. Then share what you learned.
steps.prompt: I'm planning a 2-week sprint. Team capacity: 40 hours. Tasks: Feature X (16h, high priority, depends on API being ready), Bug fixes (8h, medium), Documentation (4h, low), Code review (6h, ongoing). Need 6h slack for unexpected issues. Create a sprint plan with day-by-day allocation and flag any risks.

steps.title: Build Your PM Prompt Library
steps.body:
Create a reusable prompt template for one of these:
- Weekly status report generator
- Sprint retrospective facilitator
- Stakeholder update email writer
- Risk register auto-populator

Save it as a text snippet or note that you can reuse. This becomes part of your AI operator toolkit.
steps.deliverable: One reusable project management prompt template, ready for your next sprint.
steps.template:
Create a reusable prompt template for one of these:
- Weekly status report generator
- Sprint retrospective facilitator
- Stakeholder update email writer
- Risk register auto-populator

Save it as a text snippet or note that you can reuse. This becomes part of your AI operator toolkit.

steps.title: Paste Your PM Prompt
steps.body: Paste the prompt template you just built. This becomes a reference you can revisit anytime.
steps.prompt: Paste your reusable project management prompt here. This is your PM toolkit — you'll use it again.

steps.title: The AI PM Mindset
steps.body:
Think about a project you're currently working on or have recently completed. How could AI have changed the outcome? What specific tasks would you delegate to AI? What still requires human judgment?

Write down three things AI could help with and one thing that must remain human-led.

questions.question: Which statement about AI in project management is most accurate?
options: AI eliminates the need for project managers entirely
AI is best used for automating status updates and basic reporting only
AI excels at analyzing project data, predicting risks, and generating reports, but human judgment remains essential
AI works best when given no context so it can be creative

steps.title: Day 22 Complete: PM with AI
steps.body:
You've learned to treat AI as your project management partner — from sprint planning to risk assessment to stakeholder reporting.

Remember: the best AI operators don't just track tasks. They predict problems, automate reporting, and free themselves to focus on leadership.

Tomorrow: Financial Intelligence with AI. Get ready to make AI your CFO.
